calendar.matters:
Notice Of Renewed Petition And Petition To Compel Arbitration And For A Stay Of Dlse Proceedings
Off Calendar Sep-25-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Bichsel, Theresa (TBichsel@dir.ca.gov) for the Labor Commission
Sophia Behnia (SBehnia@littler.com) and Anthony Ly (ALy@littler.com) for Uber
Eric Guillermo-Jimenez, Respondent.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PETITIONER Uber Technologies, Inc's "renewed petition to compel arbitration and for a stay of DSLE proceedings" is granted.
The issue here is whether Uber drivers are a class of workers engaged in interstate commerce and thus their claims need not be arbitrated as they agreed. (9 U.S.C. sec. 1.)
Uber petitioned to compel arbitration in this case in November 2019. On January 24, 2020, I granted the California Labor Commissioner six months of discovery. The legal landscape has changed markedly since then.
On April 7, 2020, the Northern District of California compelled arbitration in a case, holding that drivers for Uber rideshare rival Lyft "as a class are not engaged in interstate commerce" and thus do not fit the 9 U.S.C. sec. 1 exemption. (Rogers v. Lyft, Inc., 2020 WL 1684151 *5-6, 10.)
On May 14, 2020, another Northern District of California court also compelled arbitration, holding that "Uber drivers do not fall within the Section 1 exemption because they are not 'engaged in interstate commerce'." (Capriole v. Uber Technologies, Inc., 2020 WL 2563276 *9, 11.)
On July 17, 2020, in yet another case raising the issue of whether Uber drivers are a class of workers engage in interstate commerce, I too compelled arbitration. (Brower v. Uber Technologies, Inc.) I adopted the analyses and conclusions of the "well-reasoned" Capriole and Rogers decisions.
On September 4, 2020, the Ninth Circuit upheld a Central District of California court's holding that Uber drivers "who pick up and drop off passengers at airports do not fall within" the interstate commerce exception of 9 U.S.C. sec. 1. (In re Grice, 2020 WL 5268941 *1.)
Thus, the argument that Uber drivers are a class of workers engaged in interstate commerce for arbitration purposes has now been repeatedly made and unanimously rejected in California.

order signed.

Judge: Richard Ulmer, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

Petitioner Uber Technologies, Inc. Notice Of Hearing On Petitioner Uber Technologies, Inc.'S Petition To Compel Arbitration And For A Stay Of Dlse Proceedings
Off Calendar Jan-24-2020 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances.

RULING: There being no appearance, or objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on PETITIONER UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s Petition To Compel Arbitration And For A Stay Of DLSE Proceedings, as follows:

This petition to compel arbitration is essentially the same as one in Uber v. Patel that I decided in November 2019. The order is essentially the same as in that case.

The Federal Arbitration Act does not apply to "contracts of workers?engaged in foreign or interstate commerce." (9 USC 1.) Respondent Labor Commissioner Lilia Garcia-Bower requests "limited discovery to develop facts relevant to the extent to which Uber drivers engage in interstate commerce." (See Singh v. Uber Technologies, Inc. (3rd Cir. 2019) 939 F.3d 210; Sienkaniec v. Uber Technologies, Inc. (D. Minn. 2019) No. 17-CV-4489 (PSJ/HB) 2019 WL 4308555, at *1.) Commissioner Garcia-Bower's request is granted and Uber Technologies, Inc.'s petition to compel arbitration is ordered off calendar. The limited-discovery period shall end on May 22, 2020. Uber may then re-calendar its petition for hearing. The parties may request supplemental briefing at that time. The proceedings are otherwise stayed until further order of this court. Uber's request for sanctions is denied.

Order signed after hearing.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
